AI.motivation: To investigate the dimensionality-accuracy tradeoff in embedding-based learning and understand its fundamental limits.
AI.method: The authors analyze embedding methods under contrastive learning with triplet-based distance comparisons and prove information-theoretic and computational hardness results.
AI.result: A sharp drop in accuracy occurs if the embedding dimension is below a critical threshold relative to the true dimensionality, and achieving higher accuracy is computationally hard under the Unique Games Conjecture.
AI.conclusion: Understanding the tradeoff between dimension and accuracy is critical; lower-dimensional embeddings face inherent limitations in faithfully representing data relationships.

title: A-CODE: Fully Atomic Protein Co-Design with Unified Multimodal Diffusion
summary: We present A-CODE, a fully atomic unified one-stage protein co-design model that simultaneously refines discrete atom types and continuous atom coordinates. Unlike predominant two-stage methods that cascade structure design with amino acid-level sequence design, our approach is fully atomic within a unified multimodal diffusion framework, in which residue identities are inferred solely from atom-level predictions. Built upon the powerful all-atom architecture, A-CODE achieves superior designability for unconditional protein generation, outperforming all existing one-stage and two-stage design models. For binder design, A-CODE rivals and even outperforms existing state-of-the-art two-stage design models and, compared with the existing one-stage co-design model, achieves a drastic tenfold improvement in success rate on hard tasks. The inherent flexibility of our atomic formulation enables, for the first time, seamless adaptation to non-canonical amino acid (ncAA) modeling. Our fully atomic framework establishes a new, versatile foundation for all-atom generative modeling that can be naturally extended to complex biomolecular systems.

AI.tldr: A-CODE introduces a fully atomic one-stage protein co-design model utilizing multimodal diffusion for simultaneous refinement of atom types and coordinates, outperforming existing models and enabling enhanced flexibility, including non-canonical amino acid adaptation.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in existing two-stage protein co-design methods and introduce a unified, atomic framework that improves designability and flexibility.
AI.method: Utilizes a multimodal diffusion model for simultaneous prediction of atom types and coordinates, bypassing residue-level sequence inference.
AI.result: A-CODE outperforms existing one-stage and two-stage models in unconditional generation and binder design, achieving significant success in challenging tasks and allowing non-canonical amino acid modeling.
AI.conclusion: A-CODE sets a new standard for protein co-design by offering superior performance, enhanced versatility, and adaptability for complex biomolecular systems.

title: Tailored Prompts, Targeted Protection: Vulnerability-Specific LLM Analysis for Smart Contracts
summary: Smart contracts on blockchains are prone to diverse security vulnerabilities that can lead to significant financial losses due to their immutable nature. Existing detection approaches often lack flexibility across vulnerability types and rely heavily on manually crafted expert rules. In this paper, we present an LLM-based framework for practical smart contract vulnerability detection. We construct and release a large-scale dataset comprising 31,165 professionally annotated vulnerability instances collected from over 3,200 real-world projects across 15 major blockchain platforms. Our approach leverages precise AST-based context extraction and vulnerability-specific prompt design to instantiate customized detectors for 13 prevalent vulnerability categories. Experimental results demonstrate strong effectiveness, achieving an average positive recall of 0.92 and an average negative recall of 0.85, highlighting the potential of carefully engineered contextual prompting for scalable and high-precision smart contract security analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an LLM-based framework for smart contract vulnerability detection using a large annotated dataset and precise context extraction, achieving high recall rates.
AI.motivation: Smart contracts lack security due to their immutable nature and currently used detection methods are inflexible and dependent on expert rules.
AI.method: An LLM-based framework with precise AST-based context extraction and vulnerability-specific prompt design was proposed, using a large annotated dataset for detection.
AI.result: The proposed method achieved an average positive recall of 0.92 and negative recall of 0.85 across 13 vulnerability types, showing strong performance.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates the potential of contextual prompting in scalable and accurate smart contract vulnerability detection.

title: StreakMind: AI detection and analysis of satellite streaks in astronomical images with automated database integration
comment: Published in Astronomy & Astrophysics, 708, A211 (2026), DOI: 10.1051/0004-6361/202558754
summary: Artificial satellites and space debris increasingly contaminate astronomical images, affecting scientific surveys and producing large volumes of streaked exposures. Manual inspection is no longer feasible at scale, and reliable detection and characterisation of streaks has become essential for both data-quality control and the monitoring of objects in Earth orbit. We present StreakMind, an automated pipeline designed to detect Near-Earth Objects and satellite streaks in astronomical images, characterise their geometry, and cross-identify them with known orbital objects. The system integrates all inference results into a structured database suitable for large surveys. A YOLO OBB model was trained on a hybrid dataset of 2335 images and applied to processed FITS frames. Geometric refinement, inter-frame association, satellite cross-identification, and Gaussian-based confidence scoring were then used to produce final identifications stored in a relational database. Observations from La Sagra Observatory were used to develop and test the method. On the test set, the model achieved a precision of 94 percent and a recall of 97 percent. It reliably detected faint streaks, delivered consistent geometric reconstructions, and performed robust satellite cross-identification. StreakMind demonstrates strong potential for large-scale automated analysis of linear streaks produced by both Near-Earth Objects and artificial satellites, contributing to space situational awareness.

AI.tldr: StreakMind, an automated detection pipeline, proves effective in identifying and characterizing satellite and Near-Earth Object streaks in astronomical images, achieving high precision (94%) and recall (97%).
AI.motivation: The growing contamination of astronomical images by artificial satellites and space debris creates a need for automated systems to detect and analyze streaks, as manual inspection is infeasible at scale.
AI.method: The authors developed the StreakMind pipeline, leveraging a YOLO OBB model trained on 2335 hybrid images. They combined geometric refinement, inter-frame association, satellite cross-identification, and Gaussian-based scoring to analyze processed frames.
AI.result: StreakMind achieved a precision of 94% and a recall of 97% when tested on data from La Sagra Observatory, effectively detecting faint streaks and providing consistent geometric reconstruction and satellite cross-identification.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline shows promise for automating large-scale analysis of streaks from Near-Earth Objects and artificial satellites, benefiting both astronomical data quality control and space situational awareness applications.

title: Random test functions, $H^{-1}$ norm equivalence, and stochastic variational physics-informed neural networks
summary: The dual norm characterisation of weak solutions of second-order linear elliptic partial differential equations is mathematically natural but computationally intractable: evaluating the $H^{-1}$ norm of a residual requires a supremum over an infinite-dimensional function space. We prove that the $H^{-1}$ norm of any functional is equivalent to its expected squared evaluation against a random test function whose distribution depends only on the domain. Crucially, realisations of this random test function have negative Sobolev regularity for $d \geq 2$, yet this roughness is not an obstacle: averaging over the distribution exactly recovers the correct weak topology, independently of the differential operator. This equivalence introduces the notion of stochastically weak solutions, which coincide with classical weak solutions, and motivates stochastic variational physics-informed neural networks (SV-PINNs): neural networks trained by minimising an empirical approximation of the stochastic norm of the PDE residual. Although instantiated here with neural networks as trial spaces, the underlying principle is independent of the approximation architecture and suggests a broader paradigm for numerical methods based on stochastic rather than deterministic test spaces. The framework extends naturally to higher-order elliptic, parabolic and hyperbolic equations and to abstract operator equations on Hilbert spaces. As a proof of concept, we present numerical experiments on eight challenging second-order linear elliptic problems spanning high-frequency and multi-scale solutions, indefinite operators, variable coefficients, and non-standard domains, in which SV-PINNs consistently and significantly outperform standard PINNs, recovering solutions to within one percent relative error in hundreds of L-BFGS steps.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a computationally efficient approach to solving second-order linear elliptic PDEs by introducing stochastic variational physics-informed neural networks (SV-PINNs), achieving high accuracy and performance improvements over standard methods.
AI.motivation: Evaluating weak solutions of second-order linear elliptic PDEs is computationally challenging due to the intractable $H^{-1}$ norm, which requires a supremum over an infinite-dimensional space.
AI.method: The authors utilize random test functions to equivalently approximate the $H^{-1}$ norm of the residual. This leads to the introduction of stochastically weak solutions and the design of SV-PINNs, which minimize an empirical stochastic norm using neural networks.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on eight second-order linear elliptic PDE problems demonstrate that SV-PINNs significantly outperform standard PINNs, achieving solutions with less than one percent relative error in high-dimensional and complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed stochastic framework bridges the gap between computational intractability and classical weak solutions, offering a flexible and effective paradigm applicable to a wide range of PDEs and operator equations.

title: Expanding functional protein sequence space using high entropy generative models
summary: Boltzmann Machines trained on evolutionary sequence data have emerged as a powerful paradigm for the data-driven design of artificial proteins. However, the relationship between model architecture, specifically parameter density, and experimental performance remains poorly understood. Here, we investigate this relationship using the Chorismate Mutase enzyme family as a model system. We compare standard fully connected Boltzmann Machines for Direct Coupling Analysis (bmDCA) with sparse models generated via progressive edge activation (eaDCA) and edge decimation (edDCA). We identify a maximum-entropy model (meDCA) along the decimation trajectory that represents an optimal balance between constraint satisfaction and the flexibility of the probability distribution. We synthesized and tested artificial sequences from all models using an in vivo complementation assay, finding that all architectures, regardless of sparsity, generate functional enzymes with high success rates, even at significant divergence from natural sequences. Despite this functional equivalence, we demonstrate that the meDCA model samples a viable sequence space that is more than fifteen orders of magnitude larger than its low-entropy counterparts. Furthermore, comparative analyses reveal that high-entropy models systematically minimize overfitting and better capture the local neutral spaces surrounding natural proteins. These findings suggest that while various models satisfying coevolutionary statistics can generate functional sequences, high-entropy Boltzmann Machines provide a superior representation of the underlying evolutionary fitness landscape.

AI.tldr: Boltzmann Machines are used for artificial protein design; all tested model architectures generate functional enzymes, but high-entropy models better represent evolutionary fitness landscapes.
AI.motivation: To explore how model architecture, specifically parameter density, influences experimental performance in artificial protein design.
AI.method: Comparative analysis of different Boltzmann Machine architectures (bmDCA, eaDCA, edDCA, and meDCA) and testing artificial sequences in vivo.
AI.result: All models produced functional enzymes successfully, but high-entropy models sampled larger viable sequence spaces and minimized overfitting.
AI.conclusion: High-entropy Boltzmann Machines offer superior representation of evolutionary fitness landscapes and neutral spaces, enhancing artificial protein design.

title: Deco: Extending Personal Physical Objects into Pervasive AI Companion through a Dual-Embodiment Framework
summary: Individuals frequently form deep attachments to physical objects (e.g., plush toys) that usually cannot sense or respond to their emotions. While AI companions offer responsiveness and personalization, they exist independently of these physical objects and lack an ongoing connection to them. To bridge this gap, we conducted a formative study (N=9) to explore how digital agents could inherit and extend the emotional bond, deriving four design principles (Faithful Identity, Calibrated Agency, Ambient Presence, and Reciprocal Memory). We then present the Dual-Embodiment Companion Framework, instantiated as Deco, a mobile system integrating multimodal Large Language Models (LLMs) and Augmented Reality to create synchronized digital embodiments of users' physical companions. A within-subjects study (N=25) showed Deco significantly outperformed a personalized LLM-empowered digital companion baseline on perceived companionship, emotional bond, and design-principle scales (all p<0.01). A seven-day field deployment (N=17) showed sustained engagement, subjective well-being improvement (p=.040), and three key relational patterns: digital activities retroactively vitalized physical objects, bond deepening was driven by emotional engagement depth rather than interaction frequency, and users sustained bonds while actively navigating digital companions' AI nature. This work highlights a promising alternative for designing digital companions: moving from creating new relationships to dual embodiment, where digital agents seamlessly extend the emotional history of physical objects.

AI.tldr: This study proposes a new approach for digital companions that extend the emotional bonds with physical objects users cherish, validated through a framework and system (Deco) using LLMs and AR.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between emotional attachment to physical objects and the independent nature of AI companions, aiming to create a more integrated emotional bond.
AI.method: The study involved a formative study deriving design principles, developing the Dual-Embodiment Companion Framework, creating a system (Deco) integrating multimodal LLMs and AR, and validating it through a within-subjects study and a seven-day field deployment.
AI.result: Deco significantly outperformed a baseline on companionship and emotional bond (p<0.01), showed sustained engagement, improved subjective well-being (p=.040), and revealed patterns in digital-physical relationship dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The Dual-Embodiment Companion approach offers a promising alternative by extending emotional connections with physical objects rather than creating new ones, fostering deeper and more meaningful digital companionship experiences.

title: Leveraging Code Automorphisms for Improved Syndrome-Based Neural Decoding
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, submitted to IEEE for possible publication. Code to reproduce all results is available at: https://github.com/lebidan/sbnd
summary: Syndrome-based neural decoding (SBND) has emerged as a promising deep learning approach for soft-decision decoding of high-rate, short-length codes. However, this approach still has substantial room for improvement. In this paper, we show how to leverage code automorphisms to enhance the ability of existing SBND models to learn and generalize through data augmentation during training and inference. As a result, for the short high-rate codes considered, we obtain models that closely approach MLD performance using small datasets and proper training. Our findings also suggest that many prior results for SBND models in the literature underestimate their true correction capability due to undertraining. Code to reproduce all results is available at: https://github.com/lebidan/sbnd.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes enhancements to syndrome-based neural decoding (SBND) for high-rate, short-length codes using code automorphisms for data augmentation during training and inference.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to improve the training and generalization capabilities of SBND models, which currently perform suboptimally for decoding high-rate, short-length codes.
AI.method: Code automorphisms are employed for data augmentation to enhance SBND models during training and inference.
AI.result: Improved SBND models approach Maximum Likelihood Decoding (MLD) performance using small datasets and proper training. Previous results may have underestimated SBND models' capabilities due to undertraining.
AI.conclusion: Integrating code automorphisms into SBND models leads to significant performance improvements, demonstrating near-MLD accuracy with efficient training setups.

title: Exact and Approximate Algorithms for Polytree Learning
summary: Polytrees are a subclass of Bayesian networks that seek to capture the conditional dependencies between a set of $n$ variables as a directed forest and are motivated by their more efficient inference and improved interpretability. Since the problem of learning the best polytree is NP-hard, we study which restrictions make it more tractable by considering for example in-degree bounds, properties of score functions measuring the quality of a polytree, and approximation algorithms. We devise an algorithm that finds the optimal polytree in time $O((2+ε)^n)$ for arbitrarily small $ε> 0$ and any constant in-degree bound $k$, improving over the fastest previously known algorithm of time complexity $O(3^n)$. We further give polynomial-time algorithms for finding a polytree whose score is within a factor of $k$ from the optimal one for arbitrary scores and a factor of $2$ for additive ones. Many of the results are complemented by (nearly) tight lower bounds for either the time complexity or the approximation factors.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on learning optimal polytrees, which are a subclass of Bayesian networks. It proposes faster algorithms and explores their constraints and approximations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the computational challenge of learning the optimal polytrees due to the NP-hard nature of the problem, while improving efficiency and approximations.
AI.method: The authors develop algorithms, including an $O((2+ε)^n)$ algorithm for small $ε$, surpassing prior computational benchmarks. They also offer polynomial-time approximation algorithms.
AI.result: The proposed methods achieve faster computation times and provide tight bounds for approximation factors, improving the efficiency of polytree learning.
AI.conclusion: The study contributes efficient algorithms for polytree learning, exploring restrictions and offering advancements in both exact and approximate methods.

title: PHALAR: Phasors for Learned Musical Audio Representations
summary: Stem retrieval, the task of matching missing stems to a given audio submix, is a key challenge currently limited by models that discard temporal information. We introduce PHALAR, a contrastive framework achieving a relative accuracy increase of up to $\approx 70\%$ over the state-of-the-art while requiring $<50\%$ of the parameters and a 7$\times$ training speedup. By utilizing a Learned Spectral Pooling layer and a complex-valued head, PHALAR enforces pitch-equivariant and phase-equivariant biases. PHALAR establishes new retrieval state-of-the-art across MoisesDB, Slakh, and ChocoChorales, correlating significantly higher with human coherence judgment than semantic baselines. Finally, zero-shot beat tracking and linear chord probing confirm that PHALAR captures robust musical structures beyond the retrieval task.

AI.tldr: PHALAR is a novel framework for stem retrieval, improving accuracy by up to 70% with fewer parameters and faster training compared to state-of-the-art models.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to stem retrieval discard important temporal information, limiting their effectiveness and accuracy.
AI.method: PHALAR employs a Learned Spectral Pooling layer and a complex-valued head to enforce pitch-equivariant and phase-equivariant biases, creating a contrastive learning framework.
AI.result: PHALAR achieves significant improvements in retrieval accuracy across multiple datasets (MoisesDB, Slakh, and ChocoChorales), delivers strong correlation with human judgment, and demonstrates robustness through zero-shot beat tracking and linear chord probing.
AI.conclusion: PHALAR sets a new benchmark for stem retrieval, combining higher efficacy, faster training, fewer parameters, and robust musical structure understanding.

title: Magic-Informed Quantum Architecture Search
summary: Nonstabilizerness, commonly referred to as magic, is a fundamental resource underpinning quantum advantage. In this paper, we propose a magic-informed quantum architecture search (QAS) technique that enables control over a quantum resource within the general framework of circuit design. Inspired by the AlphaGo approach, we tackle the problem with a Monte Carlo Tree Search technique equipped with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) that estimates the magic of candidate quantum circuits. The GNN model induces a magic-based bias that steers the search toward either high- or low-magic regimes, depending on the target objective. We benchmark the proposed magic-informed QAS technique on both the structured ground-state energy problem and on the more general quantum state approximation problem, spanning different sizes and target magic levels. Experimental results show that the proposed technique effectively influences the magic across the search tree and notably also on the resulting final circuit, even in regimes where the GNN operates on out-of-distribution instances. Although introducing a problem-agnostic magic bias could, in principle, constrain the search dynamics, we observe consistent improvements in solution quality across all problems tested.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quantum architecture search technique using a Monte Carlo Tree Search combined with a Graph Neural Network to control nonstabilizerness (magic) in quantum circuits. This method optimizes circuits for high or low magic while improving solution quality.
AI.motivation: To optimize quantum circuit design by leveraging nonstabilizerness (magic), a vital quantum resource for quantum advantage.
AI.method: The authors use a Monte Carlo Tree Search enhanced with a Graph Neural Network that predicts the magic level of quantum circuits to guide circuit design towards the desired magic regime.
AI.result: The method successfully adjusts magic levels of circuits and achieves consistent quality improvements in quantum tasks like ground-state energy estimation and quantum state approximation.
AI.conclusion: Magic-informed techniques in QAS enable better control of quantum resources, enhancing problem-solving capabilities in diverse scenarios.

title: Towards Open World Sound Event Detection
comment: 32 pages, 3 figures. Submitted to Signal Processing (Elsevier)
summary: Sound Event Detection (SED) plays a vital role in audio understanding, with applications in surveillance, smart cities, healthcare, and multimedia indexing. However, conventional SED systems operate under a closed-world assumption, limiting their effectiveness in real-world environments where novel acoustic events frequently emerge. Inspired by the success of open-world learning in computer vision, we introduce the Open-World Sound Event Detection (OW-SED) paradigm, where models must detect known events, identify unseen ones, and incrementally learn from them. To tackle the unique challenges of OW-SED, such as overlapping and ambiguous events, we propose a 1D Deformable architecture that leverages deformable attention to adaptively focus on salient temporal regions. Furthermore, we design a novel Open-World Deformable Sound Event Detection Transformer (WOOT) framework incorporating feature disentanglement to separate class-specific and class-agnostic representations, together with a one-to-many matching strategy and a diversity loss to enhance representation diversity. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves marginally superior performance compared to existing leading techniques in closed-world settings and significantly improves over existing baselines in open-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the OW-SED paradigm and a novel WOOT framework for handling open-world sound event detection.
AI.motivation: Conventional SED models fail to address real-world challenges due to their closed-world assumption, necessitating open-world solutions.
AI.method: The proposed 1D Deformable architecture incorporates deformable attention, coupled with the WOOT framework featuring feature disentanglement, one-to-many matching, and diversity loss.
AI.result: Experimental results show superior performance in closed-world settings and significant improvements in open-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The WOOT framework effectively addresses challenges in OW-SED, paving the way for more adaptable SED systems in real-world environments.

title: Inconsistent Databases and Argumentation Frameworks with Collective Attacks
comment: This is a pre-print of the paper accepted at the Knowledge Engineering Review journal
summary: The connection between subset-maximal repairs for inconsistent databases involving various integrity constraints and acceptable sets of arguments within argumentation frameworks has recently drawn growing interest. In this paper, we contribute to this domain by establishing a new connection when integrity constraints (ICs) include denial constraints and local-as-view tuple-generating dependencies. It turns out that SET-based Argumentation Frameworks (SETAFs), an extension of Dung's argumentation frameworks (AFs) allowing collective attacks, are needed. It is known that subset-maximal repairs under denial constraints correspond to the naive extensions, which also coincide with the preferred and stable extensions in the resulting SETAFs. Our main findings establish that repairs under the considered fragment of tuple-generating dependencies correspond to the preferred extensions. Moreover, for these dependencies, additional preprocessing allows computing a unique extension that is stable and naive. Allowing both types of constraints breaks this relationship, and even the pre-processing does not help as only preferred semantics captures these repairs. Finally, while it is known that functional dependencies do not require set-based attacks, we prove the same regarding inclusion dependencies. Thus, one can translate inconsistent databases under these restricted classes of ICs to plain AFs with attacks only between arguments.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how subset-maximal repairs for inconsistent databases, concerning denial constraints and tuple-generating dependencies, correspond to extensions in SET-based Argumentation Frameworks (SETAFs).
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in connecting database repairs for integrity constraints with argumentation frameworks to gain deeper theoretical insights and solve computational problems.
AI.method: The authors analyze the correspondence between subset-maximal repairs for inconsistent databases and various semantics (naive, preferred, stable) of SETAFs, as well as how preprocessing impacts this correspondence.
AI.result: Repairs under tuple-generating dependencies correspond to preferred extensions. Additional preprocessing can yield a unique extension that is stable and naive, provided only tuple-generating dependencies are involved. With both denial and tuple-generating dependencies, only preferred semantics capture the repairs.
AI.conclusion: This study highlights the precise nature of the relationships between database repairs under specific ICs and argumentation frameworks, allowing better computational translation between inconsistent databases and AFs or SETAFs.

title: Towards accurate extreme event likelihoods from diffusion model climate emulators
summary: ML climate model emulators are useful for scenario planning and adaptation, allowing for cost-efficient experimentation. Recently, the diffusion model Climate in a Bottle (cBottle) has been proposed for generation of atmospheric states compatible with boundary conditions of solar position and sea surface temperatures. Crucially, cBottle can be guided to generate extreme events such as Tropical Cyclones (TCs) over locations of interest. Diffusion models such as cBottle work by approximating the probability density of the training data. Here, we show use cases of the probability density estimates of atmospheric states obtained from this climate emulator. Most importantly, these estimates allow us to calculate likelihoods of extreme events under guidance. When guiding the model towards states including TCs, comparing the probability density under the guided and unguided model enables us to quantify how much more likely the guidance has made the TC. We show how these odds ratios allow us to importance-sample from the TC distribution, reducing the standard error of the probability estimate compared to simple Monte Carlo sampling. Furthermore, we discuss results and limitations of the application of model probability densities to extreme event attribution-like experiments. We present these early but encouraging results hoping they will spur more research into probabilistic information that can be gained from diffusion models of the atmosphere.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the use of the Climate in a Bottle (cBottle) diffusion model to analyze and estimate the probability density of extreme atmospheric events like Tropical Cyclones (TCs).
AI.motivation: Understanding the likelihood and behavior of extreme climate events is critical for scenario planning and adaptation. There is a need for more cost-efficient and accurate tools to estimate these events.
AI.method: The authors use the cBottle diffusion model, which approximates atmospheric probability densities and supports guided generation of extreme weather events. The approach also enables calculating odds ratios to quantify the influence of guidance and applying importance sampling to reduce standard error in probability estimates.
AI.result: The study demonstrates how probability density estimates can quantify the likelihood of guided events, improve Monte Carlo sampling, and support experiments like extreme event attribution.
AI.conclusion: The authors find that probabilistic insights from diffusion models like cBottle have promising applications for understanding and estimating climate extremes, although some limitations exist. The paper aims to encourage further exploration in this area.

title: Physics-Grounded Multi-Agent Architecture for Traceable, Risk-Aware Human-AI Decision Support in Manufacturing
summary: High-precision CNC machining of free-form aerospace components requires bounded compensations informed by inspection, simulation, and process knowledge. Off-the-shelf large language model (LLM) assistants can generate text, but they do not reliably execute risk-constrained multi-step numerical workflows or provide auditable provenance for high-stakes decisions. We present multi-agent knowledge analysis (MAKA), a human-in-the-loop decision-support architecture that separates intent routing, tools-only quantitative analysis, knowledge graph retrieval, and critic-based verification that enforces physical plausibility, safety bounds, and provenance completeness before recommendations are surfaced for human approval. MAKA is instantiated on a Ti-6Al-4V rotor blade machining testbed by fusing virtual-machining path-tracking error fields, cutting-force and deflection simulations, and scan-based 3D inspection deviation maps from 16 blades. The analysis decomposes deviation into an evidence-linked pathing component, a drift-based wear proxy capturing systematic evolution across parts, a residual systematic compliance term, and a variability proxy for instability-aware escalation. In a three-level tool-orchestration benchmark (single-step through $\geq$3-step stateful sequences), MAKA improves successful tool execution by up to 87.5 percentage points relative to an unstructured single-model interaction pattern with identical tool access. Digital twin what-if studies show MAKA can coordinate traceable compensation candidates that reduce predicted surface deviation from order $10^{-2}$in to approximately $\pm 10^{-3}$in over most of the blade within the simulation environment, providing a pre-deployment verification signal for risk-aware human decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a human-in-the-loop system called MAKA, improving CNC machining decision support by ensuring traceable and verifiable results through multi-agent coordination.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges in high-precision CNC machining where generic language models fail in executing complex, risk-constrained and verifiable workflows.
AI.method: MAKA architecture integrates intent routing, multi-agent analysis, knowledge graph retrieval, and critic-based verification for physical and safety compliance prior to human approval.
AI.result: MAKA improves tool execution success rates by up to 87.5% and significantly reduces predicted surface deviations in simulations, enhancing precision machining outcomes.
AI.conclusion: MAKA provides a reliable decision-support framework for aerospace CNC machining by ensuring safety, provenance, and improved accuracy through simulation-driven compensations.

title: On Computing Total Variation Distance Between Mixtures of Product Distributions
summary: We study the problem of approximating the total variation distance between two mixtures of product distributions over an $n$-dimensional discrete domain. Given two mixtures $\mathbb{P}$ and $\mathbb{Q}$ with $k_1$ and $k_2$ product distributions over $[q]^n$, respectively, we give a randomized algorithm that approximates $d_{\mathrm{TV}}\left({\mathbb{P}},{\mathbb{Q}}\right)$ within a multiplicative error of $(1\pm \varepsilon)$ in time $\mathrm{poly}((nq)^{k_1+k_2},1/\varepsilon)$. We also study the special case of mixtures of Boolean subcubes over $\{0,1\}^n$. For this class, we give a deterministic algorithm that exactly computes the total variation distance in time $\mathrm{poly}(n,2^{O(k_1+k_2)})$, and show that exact computation is $\#\mathsf{P}$-hard when $k_1+k_2=Θ(n)$.

AI.tldr: This paper presents algorithms to approximate and compute the total variation distance between mixtures of product distributions, over discrete and Boolean domains, with specific results on efficiency and computational complexity.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in understanding the total variation distance between complex distributions, which is a fundamental measure for quantifying differences between probability distributions, especially useful in data science, statistics, and machine learning.
AI.method: The authors introduce a randomized algorithm for approximation within a multiplicative error and a deterministic algorithm for exact computation in specific cases, relying on polynomial time complexity concerning input dimensions and mixture parameters.
AI.result: The algorithms achieve efficient approximation and exact computation for the total variation distance between mixtures of product distributions. Approximation works in polynomial time, while exact computation is shown to be #P-hard when the mixture size scales linearly with the dimension.
AI.conclusion: Efficient algorithms are provided for both approximating and computing the total variation distance, but the complexity escalates in certain cases, highlighting computational limits.

title: Graph Neural Networks in the Wilson Loop Representation of Abelian Lattice Gauge Theories
summary: Local gauge structures play a central role in a wide range of condensed matter systems and synthetic quantum platforms, where they emerge as effective descriptions of strongly correlated phases and engineered dynamics. We introduce a gauge-invariant graph neural network (GNN) architecture for Abelian lattice gauge models, in which symmetry is enforced explicitly through local gauge-invariant inputs, such as Wilson loops, and preserved throughout message passing, eliminating redundant gauge degrees of freedom while retaining expressive power. We benchmark the approach on both $\mathbb{Z}_2$ and $\mathrm{U}(1)$ lattice gauge models, achieving accurate predictions of global observables and spatially resolved quantities despite the nonlocal correlations induced by gauge-matter coupling. We further demonstrate that the learned model serves as an efficient surrogate for semiclassical dynamics in $\mathrm{U}(1)$ quantum link models, enabling stable and scalable time evolution without repeated fermionic diagonalization, while faithfully reproducing both local dynamics and statistical correlations. These results establish gauge-invariant message passing as a compact and physically grounded framework for learning and simulating Abelian lattice gauge systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a gauge-invariant graph neural network (GNN) for Abelian lattice gauge models, leveraging local gauge-invariant inputs to predict observables and simulate semiclassical dynamics efficiently.
AI.motivation: Understanding condensed matter systems and synthetic quantum platforms requires robust tools to analyze gauge symmetries central to their behaviors.
AI.method: The authors develop a GNN that explicitly enforces gauge symmetries through local gauge-invariant inputs like Wilson loops, maintaining these symmetries during message passing to optimize data representation.
AI.result: The GNN architecture accurately predicts global and spatially resolved observables in lattice gauge models, and serves as an efficient tool for semiclassical dynamics simulations without computational overhead from repeated diagonalizations.
AI.conclusion: Gauge-invariant message passing schemes offer a physically grounded and efficient framework to analyze and simulate dynamics in Abelian lattice gauge systems.

title: Ecologically-Constrained Task Arithmetic for Multi-Taxa Bioacoustic Classifiers Without Shared Data
summary: Training data for bioacoustics is scattered across taxa, regions, and institutions. Centralizing it all is often infeasible. We show that independently fine-tuned BEATs encoders can be composed into a unified 661-species classifier via task vector arithmetic without sharing data. We find that bioacoustic task vectors are near-orthogonal (cosine 0.01-0.09). Their separation aligns closely with spectral distribution distance, a gradient consistent with the acoustic niche hypothesis. This geometry makes simple averaging optimal while sign-conflict methods reduce accuracy by one to six percentage points. Composition also creates an asymmetric gap: species-rich groups lose accuracy relative to joint training while underrepresented taxa gain, a redistribution useful for equitable biodiversity monitoring. We verify linear mode connectivity across all taxonomic pairs, demonstrate zero-shot transfer to new regions, and identify domain negation as a boundary condition where composition fails. These results enable a collaborative paradigm for bioacoustics where institutions share only task vectors to assemble multi-taxa classifiers, preserving data privacy.

AI.tldr: The study presents a method to create a unified species classifier by combining independently fine-tuned BEATs encoders using task vector arithmetic, avoiding direct data sharing.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the difficulty of centralizing bioacoustics training data across diverse taxa, regions, and institutions, compounded by data privacy concerns.
AI.method: The authors employ task vector arithmetic to combine fine-tuned BEATs encoders into a unified classifier, analyzing vector geometries and their implications for accuracy and representation.
AI.result: Bioacoustic task vectors exhibit near-orthogonality, optimizing simple averaging for composition. Species-rich taxa see accuracy loss compared to joint training, but underrepresented taxa benefit. The method supports zero-shot transfer and highlights domain negation as a failure condition.
AI.conclusion: The approach supports a collaborative paradigm for bioacoustics, enabling institutions to share task vectors instead of data, thus addressing privacy concerns while advancing multi-taxa classification.

title: Exact ReLU realization of tensor-product refinement iterates
summary:
We study scalar dyadic refinement operators on R^2 of the form (Vf)(x,y) = sum_{(j,k) in Z^2} c_{j,k} f(2x-j, 2y-k), where only finitely many mask coefficients c_{j,k} are nonzero. Under a fixed support-window hypothesis, we prove that for every compactly supported continuous piecewise linear seed g:R^2->R, the iterates V^n g admit exact ReLU realizations of fixed width and depth O(n).
  This gives a first genuinely two-dimensional extension of the exact realization theory for refinement cascades. Using the one-dimensional exact loop-controller framework, the proof transports the tensor-product residual dynamics exactly on the product of two polygonal loops and reduces the remaining seam ambiguity to a final readout and selector step. The matrix cascade is then handled by a fixed-depth recursive block, and general compactly supported continuous piecewise linear seeds are reduced to a finite decomposition together with exact clamped gluing on the support window. This identifies the tensor-product dyadic case as a natural first multivariate instance of the loop-controller method for refinement iterates.

AI.tldr: The paper examines refinement operators on R^2 applied to compactly supported continuous piecewise linear seeds, proving their exact realizations using neural networks with fixed width and depth scaling as O(n).
AI.motivation: To extend the exact realization theory for refinement cascades into a two-dimensional setting that aligns with neural network architectures.
AI.method: Under a fixed support-window hypothesis, it uses the tensor-product residual dynamics for exact ReLU realizations while resolving seam ambiguities via selector steps and recursive depth reduction.
AI.result: Exact ReLU realizations of refinement iterates are achieved, demonstrating fixed-width and depth scalability, and identifying tensor-product dyadic refinement as a model for multivariate refinement theories.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully bridges scalar dyadic operators and neural network realizations, offering insights into multivariate approaches for exact realization theories.
